questions.question: What is the Repository pattern and why is it used?
questions.answer:
The Repository pattern abstracts data access behind an interface — it decouples your business logic from the persistence mechanism (EF Core, Dapper, API, etc.).

public interface IPortfolioRepository
{
    Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task<IReadOnlyList<Portfolio>> GetByUserAsync(string userId, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task AddAsync(Portfolio portfolio, CancellationToken ct = default);
    Task DeleteAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default);
}

public class EfPortfolioRepository : IPortfolioRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public EfPortfolioRepository(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    public async Task<Portfolio?> GetByIdAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct = default)
        => await _db.Portfolios.AsNoTracking()
               .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id, ct);

    public async Task AddAsync(Portfolio portfolio, CancellationToken ct = default)
    {
        _db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    }
    // ...
}

Benefits: Testable (mock the interface), swappable data source, single place for all data access logic for an entity.

The controversy: EF Core's DbContext IS already a repository + Unit of Work. For small-medium apps, adding another layer is over-engineering. I use it when I need testability without hitting the real DB, or when mixing EF Core + Dapper for the same entity.
followUps: What is Unit of Work pattern?
Is Repository pattern needed with EF Core?

followUpAnswers.What is Unit of Work pattern?:
The Unit of Work (UoW) pattern tracks all changes made during a business transaction across multiple repositories and flushes them to the database as a single atomic operation. It prevents partial writes — either all changes persist or none do.

Responsibilities of a Unit of Work:

  1. Keep track of entity changes (added, modified, deleted) across multiple operations
  2. Coordinate writes to multiple repositories within a single database transaction
  3. Provide a single Commit() / SaveChanges() point
// Explicit UoW interface
public interface IUnitOfWork
{
    IPortfolioRepository Portfolios { get; }
    IHoldingRepository Holdings { get; }
    Task<int> CommitAsync(CancellationToken ct = default);
}

public class AppUnitOfWork : IUnitOfWork
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;

    public AppUnitOfWork(AppDbContext db)
    {
        _db = db;
        Portfolios = new EfPortfolioRepository(db);
        Holdings = new EfHoldingRepository(db);
    }

    public IPortfolioRepository Portfolios { get; }
    public IHoldingRepository Holdings { get; }

    // Single commit for all repository changes
    public Task<int> CommitAsync(CancellationToken ct = default)
        => _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
}

// Business logic uses UoW — repositories share the same DbContext
public async Task CreatePortfolioWithInitialHoldingAsync(CreateRequest req)
{
    var portfolio = new Portfolio { UserId = req.UserId, Name = req.Name };
    await _uow.Portfolios.AddAsync(portfolio);

    var holding = new Holding { PortfolioId = portfolio.Id, Symbol = req.Symbol };
    await _uow.Holdings.AddAsync(holding);

    await _uow.CommitAsync();  // both written atomically
}

EF Core's DbContext IS a Unit of Work — it already tracks changes and commits them all in SaveChangesAsync(). A custom IUnitOfWork wrapping DbContext adds value mainly when you want your domain logic completely decoupled from EF Core.
followUpAnswers.Is Repository pattern needed with EF Cor:
This is a genuinely debated topic. The honest answer: it depends on your testing strategy and architecture goals.

Arguments for skipping the Repository pattern with EF Core:

  • DbContext IS a repository — DbSet<T> has Add, Find, Remove. Adding a wrapper is a redundant abstraction.
  • IQueryable<T> is already a query abstraction — you lose composability if you hide it behind IEnumerable<T> in your repository interface.
  • EF Core InMemory and SQLite in-memory providers let you test without mocking — no repository interface needed for testability.
  • The abstraction leaks anyway — EF Core-specific concepts (AsNoTracking, Include, projections) end up referenced in the service layer or bloating the repository.

Arguments for keeping the Repository pattern:

  • You want to swap the data layer entirely (EF Core → Dapper → external API) without changing service code.
  • You want fast unit tests with Mock<IPortfolioRepository> — no database at all, even in-memory.
  • Your domain logic has complex business rules you want to test in total isolation from data access.
  • Team convention — consistent pattern across a large codebase is easier to maintain.
// Without repository: use DbContext directly in service
public class PortfolioService
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public PortfolioService(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    public async Task<Portfolio?> GetAsync(int id, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Portfolios.AsNoTracking()
               .FirstOrDefaultAsync(p => p.Id == id, ct);
}
// Test with SQLite in-memory — no mocking needed

My recommendation: For CRUD-heavy apps, skip it and use DbContext directly with in-memory SQLite for tests. Add the abstraction when you genuinely need to swap data sources or isolate domain logic.

questions.tip: Generic repository anti-pattern: IRepository<T> with just CRUD methods encourages leaking data access logic into the service layer (callers end up building queries themselves). Prefer entity-specific repositories (IPortfolioRepository) with meaningful methods.

questions.question: What is the Strategy pattern and how do you implement it in C#?
questions.answer:
The Strategy pattern defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each one, and makes them interchangeable. The behavior can be changed at runtime without changing the class that uses it.

// Strategy interface
public interface IPaymentStrategy
{
    string Name { get; }
    Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct);
}

// Concrete strategies
public class StripePaymentStrategy : IPaymentStrategy
{
    public string Name => "stripe";
    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    { /* Stripe API call */ }
}

public class RazorpayPaymentStrategy : IPaymentStrategy
{
    public string Name => "razorpay";
    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    { /* Razorpay API call */ }
}

// Context that uses the strategy
public class PaymentService
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> _strategies;
    public PaymentService(IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> strategies)
        => _strategies = strategies;

    public async Task<PaymentResult> PayAsync(string provider, decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var strategy = _strategies.SingleOrDefault(s => s.Name == provider)
            ?? throw new ArgumentException($"Unknown provider: {provider}");
        return await strategy.ProcessAsync(amount, ct);
    }
}

// DI registration — all strategies injected
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentStrategy, StripePaymentStrategy>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentStrategy, RazorpayPaymentStrategy>();
followUps: How does Strategy differ from Factory pattern?
How is Strategy related to OCP?

followUpAnswers.How does Strategy differ from Factory pa:
Strategy and Factory are often confused because both involve selecting between multiple implementations. The distinction is about purpose:

StrategyFactory
PurposeChoose HOW to do something (algorithm/behavior)Choose WHAT to create (object creation)
FocusRuntime behavior substitutionObject instantiation encapsulation
ReturnsA behavior to executeA new object instance
Object lifecycleStrategy is typically long-lived or injectedFactory creates and returns; may not keep reference
// STRATEGY: selecting HOW to process a payment
public class PaymentService
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> _strategies;

    public async Task<Result> PayAsync(string provider, decimal amount)
    {
        // Select strategy at runtime — the strategy IS the behavior
        var strategy = _strategies.Single(s => s.Name == provider);
        return await strategy.ProcessAsync(amount);  // execute the behavior
    }
}

// FACTORY: creating WHAT kind of notification to send
public class NotificationFactory
{
    public INotification Create(string channel) => channel switch
    {
        "email" => new EmailNotification(),  // creates and returns an object
        "sms"   => new SmsNotification(),
        _        => throw new ArgumentException()
    };
}

// Usage of factory: factory creates, then you use what it created
var notification = _factory.Create("email");
await notification.SendAsync(message);

// They can work TOGETHER: Factory creates strategies
// Factory decides which strategy implementation to instantiate
var strategy = _strategyFactory.Create(paymentProvider);
await strategy.ProcessAsync(amount);
followUpAnswers.How is Strategy related to OCP?:
The Strategy pattern is one of the most direct expressions of the Open/Closed Principle (OCP): classes should be open for extension but closed for modification.

Without Strategy (OCP violation):

// BAD: adding a new payment provider requires MODIFYING PaymentService
public class PaymentService
{
    public async Task<Result> PayAsync(string provider, decimal amount)
    {
        if (provider == "stripe")
        {
            // Stripe logic
        }
        else if (provider == "razorpay")
        {
            // Razorpay logic
        }
        // ADDING PayPal requires modifying this class — OCP violated!
        else if (provider == "paypal")
        {
            // PayPal logic
        }
    }
}

With Strategy (OCP satisfied):

// GOOD: adding PayPal requires ONLY a new class + DI registration
// PaymentService is never modified
public class PayPalPaymentStrategy : IPaymentStrategy
{
    public string Name => "paypal";
    public async Task<PaymentResult> ProcessAsync(decimal amount, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // PayPal logic — PaymentService doesn't know or care
    }
}

// Registration — the only change to existing code
builder.Services.AddScoped<IPaymentStrategy, PayPalPaymentStrategy>();

The PaymentService context class is completely untouched. It's open for extension (new strategies can be added) but closed for modification (its code doesn't change). The IEnumerable<IPaymentStrategy> injection pattern is the .NET idiomatic way to achieve this — the DI container automatically picks up all registered implementations.

questions.tip: Strategy + DI = powerful combo. Register all strategies as IEnumerable<IStrategy> and inject — the container provides all implementations. Select by a discriminator property (Name, Type, etc.). Adding a new strategy is just a new class + DI registration.

questions.question: What is the Factory pattern and its variants?
questions.answer:
Factory patterns encapsulate object creation logic.

Simple Factory — a static method or class that creates objects based on some input. Not a GoF pattern but very common.

public static class NotificationFactory
{
    public static INotification Create(string type) => type switch
    {
        "email" => new EmailNotification(),
        "sms"   => new SmsNotification(),
        _        => throw new ArgumentException($"Unknown type: {type}")
    };
}

Factory Method — defines an interface for creating objects, lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate.

public abstract class ReportGenerator
{
    public abstract IReport CreateReport();  // factory method

    public void Generate()
    {
        var report = CreateReport();  // subclass decides which report
        report.Build();
        report.Export();
    }
}

public class PdfReportGenerator : ReportGenerator
{
    public override IReport CreateReport() => new PdfReport();
}

Abstract Factory — creates families of related objects. Useful for multi-platform or multi-theme scenarios.

In CSInvoice, I used a factory to create different invoice document types (PDF, Word, Excel) based on client preference — same interface, different concrete implementations selected by factory.
followUps: When would you use Factory vs Strategy?

followUpAnswers.Abstract Factory vs Factory Method?:
Both are creational patterns that abstract object creation, but they operate at different levels of abstraction:

Factory MethodAbstract Factory
LevelCreates one productCreates a family of related products
MechanismInheritance — subclass overrides the factory methodComposition — inject an abstract factory interface
When to useOne type of product, subclass decides which variantMultiple related products that must be consistent
// FACTORY METHOD: subclass decides which IReport to create
public abstract class ReportGenerator
{
    protected abstract IReport CreateReport();  // factory method
    public void Run() { CreateReport().Generate(); }
}
public class PdfGenerator : ReportGenerator
{
    protected override IReport CreateReport() => new PdfReport();
}
public class ExcelGenerator : ReportGenerator
{
    protected override IReport CreateReport() => new ExcelReport();
}

// ABSTRACT FACTORY: creates a FAMILY of related UI controls consistently
public interface IUIFactory
{
    IButton CreateButton();
    ICheckbox CreateCheckbox();
    ITextInput CreateTextInput();
}

public class WindowsUIFactory : IUIFactory
{
    public IButton CreateButton() => new WindowsButton();
    public ICheckbox CreateCheckbox() => new WindowsCheckbox();
    public ITextInput CreateTextInput() => new WindowsTextInput();
}

public class MacUIFactory : IUIFactory
{
    public IButton CreateButton() => new MacButton();
    public ICheckbox CreateCheckbox() => new MacCheckbox();
    public ITextInput CreateTextInput() => new MacTextInput();
}

// The app uses IUIFactory — never knows if it's Windows or Mac
public class LoginForm
{
    public LoginForm(IUIFactory factory)
    {
        // All controls are from the same family — visually consistent
        var button = factory.CreateButton();
        var input = factory.CreateTextInput();
    }
}
followUpAnswers.When would you use Factory vs Strategy?:
The decision comes down to what problem you're solving: creation vs behavior.

Use Factory when:

  • You need to create objects and the type depends on runtime conditions
  • The creation logic is complex and you want to centralize it
  • You want to hide constructor dependencies from callers
  • You're building a plugin or extensibility system where new types can be registered
// Factory: deciding WHAT object to create
public class ExportHandlerFactory
{
    private readonly IServiceProvider _sp;
    public ExportHandlerFactory(IServiceProvider sp) => _sp = sp;

    public IExportHandler Create(string format) => format switch
    {
        "pdf"   => _sp.GetRequiredService<PdfExportHandler>(),
        "excel" => _sp.GetRequiredService<ExcelExportHandler>(),
        "csv"   => _sp.GetRequiredService<CsvExportHandler>(),
        _ => throw new ArgumentException($"Unsupported format: {format}")
    };
}

Use Strategy when:

  • You have multiple algorithms/behaviors for the same operation
  • The behavior needs to be swappable at runtime
  • You want to avoid large if/switch blocks in business logic
  • You're implementing OCP — new behaviors without changing existing code
// Strategy: deciding HOW to execute behavior
public class ShippingService
{
    private readonly IEnumerable<IShippingStrategy> _strategies;

    public decimal CalculateCost(Order order, string carrier)
    {
        var strategy = _strategies.Single(s => s.Carrier == carrier);
        return strategy.Calculate(order);  // each strategy has different algorithm
    }
}

// Factory CREATES the strategies; Strategy DEFINES the behaviors.
// Often used together: Factory selects and creates the right Strategy.
var handler = _factory.Create(format);  // Factory creates...
handler.Export(data);                   // ...which behaves like a Strategy

questions.tip: Factory vs Strategy: Factory is about creation (what to instantiate). Strategy is about behavior (how to do something). If you're choosing an algorithm to run, use Strategy. If you're choosing an object to create, use Factory.

questions.question: What is the Decorator pattern and how do you use it in .NET?
questions.answer:
The Decorator pattern wraps an object with additional behavior without modifying the original class. It's an alternative to inheritance for adding responsibilities.

// Base interface
public interface IEmailSender
{
    Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body);
}

// Real implementation
public class SmtpEmailSender : IEmailSender
{
    public async Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body)
    { /* SMTP logic */ }
}

// Logging decorator — wraps any IEmailSender
public class LoggingEmailSender : IEmailSender
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _inner;
    private readonly ILogger _logger;

    public LoggingEmailSender(IEmailSender inner, ILogger<LoggingEmailSender> logger)
    {
        _inner = inner;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Sending email to {To}: {Subject}", to, subject);
        await _inner.SendAsync(to, subject, body);
        _logger.LogInformation("Email sent successfully to {To}", to);
    }
}

// With Scrutor library for clean DI decoration
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();
builder.Services.Decorate<IEmailSender, LoggingEmailSender>();

The Decorator pattern is how I add retry logic, caching, and logging to services without modifying them — perfect for the Open/Closed Principle.
followUps: How does Scrutor simplify Decorator registration?
What is the difference between Decorator and Proxy pattern?

followUpAnswers.How does Scrutor simplify Decorator regi:
Without Scrutor, registering a decorator in .NET's built-in DI is verbose and error-prone — you have to manually resolve the inner service and wire it up:

// WITHOUT Scrutor: manual decorator wiring (fragile)
builder.Services.AddScoped<SmtpEmailSender>();  // register concrete
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender>(sp =>
    new LoggingEmailSender(
        sp.GetRequiredService<SmtpEmailSender>(),  // manually resolve inner
        sp.GetRequiredService<ILogger<LoggingEmailSender>>()));
// Problem: if SmtpEmailSender has complex dependencies, this gets messy fast

// Stacking multiple decorators without Scrutor = pain:
// CachingEmailSender(LoggingEmailSender(RetryEmailSender(SmtpEmailSender)))
// Each layer requires manual resolution of the previous

With Scrutor (install Scrutor NuGet package):

// WITH Scrutor: clean, composable decoration
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEmailSender, SmtpEmailSender>();

// Each Decorate() call wraps the current registration
builder.Services.Decorate<IEmailSender, RetryEmailSender>();
builder.Services.Decorate<IEmailSender, LoggingEmailSender>();
builder.Services.Decorate<IEmailSender, CachingEmailSender>();

// Resolution order (outermost first):
// CachingEmailSender → LoggingEmailSender → RetryEmailSender → SmtpEmailSender

// Each decorator only needs to declare its own dependencies:
public class RetryEmailSender : IEmailSender
{
    private readonly IEmailSender _inner;  // Scrutor injects the wrapped service
    private readonly ILogger<RetryEmailSender> _logger;

    public RetryEmailSender(IEmailSender inner, ILogger<RetryEmailSender> logger)
    {
        _inner = inner;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task SendAsync(string to, string subject, string body)
    {
        for (int attempt = 1; attempt <= 3; attempt++)
        {
            try { await _inner.SendAsync(to, subject, body); return; }
            catch when (attempt < 3) { await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(attempt)); }
        }
    }
}

Scrutor also provides services.Scan() for automatic assembly scanning and registration — useful for large codebases.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Decorator:
Both Decorator and Proxy wrap another object and implement the same interface, making them look similar structurally. The difference is in intent:

DecoratorProxy
IntentADD behavior to an objectCONTROL access to an object
TransparencyBoth client and pattern author know it's a decoratorClient often doesn't know a proxy is involved
Typical useLogging, caching, retry, validation wrappingLazy loading, access control, remote proxy, protection
Knows about inner?Yes — explicitly wraps a known instanceMay create/manage the inner object itself (lazy init)
// DECORATOR: adds logging behavior — augments without controlling
public class LoggingOrderService : IOrderService
{
    private readonly IOrderService _inner;
    public LoggingOrderService(IOrderService inner) => _inner = inner;

    public async Task<Order> GetAsync(int id)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Getting order {Id}", id);
        var result = await _inner.GetAsync(id);  // always delegates
        _logger.LogInformation("Got order {Id}", id);
        return result;
    }
}

// PROXY: controls access — may block, redirect, or lazily initialize
public class AuthorizationOrderProxy : IOrderService
{
    private readonly IOrderService _inner;
    private readonly ICurrentUserService _user;

    public async Task<Order> GetAsync(int id)
    {
        // Controls WHETHER to delegate at all
        if (!_user.HasPermission("orders.read"))
            throw new UnauthorizedException();

        return await _inner.GetAsync(id);
    }
}

// VIRTUAL PROXY: lazy initialization — creates inner only when first needed
public class LazyExpensiveServiceProxy : IExpensiveService
{
    private IExpensiveService? _inner;
    public string GetData() => (_inner ??= new RealExpensiveService()).GetData();
}

questions.tip: Scrutor's Decorate<T>() is the cleanest way to register decorators in .NET DI. Without it, you have to manually resolve the inner service and wrap it — messy. Install Scrutor NuGet and use services.Decorate<IService, DecoratorService>().

questions.question: What is the Observer pattern and how is it implemented in C#?
questions.answer:
The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency — when one object (subject) changes state, all dependents (observers) are notified automatically.

Built-in .NET mechanisms for Observer:

  • Events + delegates (classic approach)
  • IObservable<T> / IObserver<T> (Rx.NET)
  • MediatR notifications (modern approach in .NET apps)
// MediatR notification (recommended in .NET apps)
public record InvoiceCreatedNotification(int InvoiceId, string ClientEmail)
    : INotification;

// Multiple handlers — all called when notification is published
public class SendReceiptEmailHandler : INotificationHandler<InvoiceCreatedNotification>
{
    public async Task Handle(InvoiceCreatedNotification notification, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _emailSender.SendReceiptAsync(notification.ClientEmail);
}

public class UpdateAnalyticsDashboardHandler : INotificationHandler<InvoiceCreatedNotification>
{
    public async Task Handle(InvoiceCreatedNotification notification, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _analytics.IncrementInvoiceCountAsync();
}

// Publisher
await _mediator.Publish(new InvoiceCreatedNotification(invoice.Id, invoice.ClientEmail));

MediatR decouples the publisher (InvoiceService) from subscribers (email handler, analytics handler) — neither knows the other exists.
followUps: What is MediatR and how does it implement CQRS?
IObservable vs events — when to use which?

followUpAnswers.What is MediatR and how does it implemen:
MediatR is a .NET library that implements the Mediator pattern — it's an in-process message bus that routes requests (commands/queries) to their handlers and publishes notifications to multiple subscribers.

How MediatR implements CQRS:

  • IRequest<T> + IRequestHandler<TRequest, TResponse> — for commands (write) and queries (read)
  • INotification + INotificationHandler<T> — for events (Observer pattern / fan-out)
// COMMAND (write side)
public record CreateInvoiceCommand(
    string ClientEmail, decimal Amount) : IRequest<int>;

public class CreateInvoiceHandler : IRequestHandler<CreateInvoiceCommand, int>
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    private readonly IMediator _mediator;

    public async Task<int> Handle(CreateInvoiceCommand cmd, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var invoice = new Invoice { ClientEmail = cmd.ClientEmail, Amount = cmd.Amount };
        _db.Invoices.Add(invoice);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);

        // Publish event after successful write
        await _mediator.Publish(
            new InvoiceCreatedNotification(invoice.Id, cmd.ClientEmail), ct);

        return invoice.Id;
    }
}

// QUERY (read side)
public record GetInvoicesQuery(string ClientEmail) : IRequest<List<InvoiceDto>>;

public class GetInvoicesHandler : IRequestHandler<GetInvoicesQuery, List<InvoiceDto>>
{
    public async Task<List<InvoiceDto>> Handle(GetInvoicesQuery q, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Invoices
            .AsNoTracking()
            .Where(i => i.ClientEmail == q.ClientEmail)
            .Select(i => new InvoiceDto(i.Id, i.Amount, i.Status))
            .ToListAsync(ct);
}

// Controller: thin, delegates to MediatR
[HttpPost]
public async Task<IActionResult> Create(CreateInvoiceRequest req, CancellationToken ct)
{
    var id = await _mediator.Send(new CreateInvoiceCommand(req.Email, req.Amount), ct);
    return CreatedAtAction(nameof(Get), new { id }, id);
}

MediatR also supports pipeline behaviors (similar to middleware) for cross-cutting concerns: validation, logging, caching, transaction management — applied to all commands/queries without touching handler code.
followUpAnswers.IObservable vs events — when to use whic:
Both implement the Observer pattern, but they have very different capabilities and ergonomics:

C# Events/DelegatesIObservable<T> (Rx.NET)
Async supportNo (async void handlers)Yes — full async stream support
Error handlingException in one handler breaks othersOnError — centralized error handling
Completion signalNo concept of stream endOnCompleted
ComposabilityVery limitedRich LINQ operators: Where, Select, Buffer, Throttle, Merge
Learning curveLowHigh
Best forSimple in-process events, UI eventsEvent streams, real-time data, complex async workflows
// C# Events: simple, synchronous, well-understood
public class OrderService
{
    public event EventHandler<OrderCreatedEventArgs>? OrderCreated;

    public Order Create(OrderRequest req)
    {
        var order = new Order(req);
        OrderCreated?.Invoke(this, new OrderCreatedEventArgs(order));
        return order;
    }
}

// IObservable: rich stream processing
// Stock price ticker — filter, buffer, throttle
IObservable<StockPrice> priceStream = GetPriceStream();

priceStream
    .Where(p => p.Symbol == "AAPL")
    .Buffer(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5))   // collect 5 seconds of prices
    .Select(batch => batch.Average(p => p.Price))  // 5-second moving average
    .Throttle(TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(100))       // debounce rapid updates
    .Subscribe(
        avg => Console.WriteLine($"AAPL 5s avg: {avg:F2}"),
        ex  => Console.WriteLine($"Error: {ex.Message}"),
        ()  => Console.WriteLine("Stream ended"));

Recommendation: Use C# events for simple in-process notifications. Use IObservable/Rx.NET for complex event stream processing with filtering, buffering, or combining multiple streams. Use MediatR for structured in-app CQRS/notification patterns.

questions.tip: MediatR Publish is synchronous by default — all handlers run in sequence. For truly async fan-out (fire-and-forget to multiple handlers), use a message bus (Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ) instead. MediatR is for in-process notifications.

questions.question: What is CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation)?
questions.answer:
CQRS separates read operations (queries) from write operations (commands) into distinct paths. Queries return data and don't change state. Commands change state and may or may not return data.

Why separate them? Read and write requirements are often different: reads need to be fast and denormalized (reporting views, dashboards), writes need validation and business rules. Separating allows independent scaling and optimization.

// Command (write) — via MediatR
public record CreatePortfolioCommand(string UserId, string Name) : IRequest<int>;

public class CreatePortfolioHandler : IRequestHandler<CreatePortfolioCommand, int>
{
    public async Task<int> Handle(CreatePortfolioCommand cmd, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Validation, business rules, then persist
        var portfolio = new Portfolio { UserId = cmd.UserId, Name = cmd.Name };
        _db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
        return portfolio.Id;
    }
}

// Query (read) — via MediatR
public record GetPortfoliosQuery(string UserId) : IRequest<List<PortfolioDto>>;

public class GetPortfoliosHandler : IRequestHandler<GetPortfoliosQuery, List<PortfolioDto>>
{
    public async Task<List<PortfolioDto>> Handle(GetPortfoliosQuery query, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // No EF tracking, straight to DTO
        return await _db.Portfolios
            .AsNoTracking()
            .Where(p => p.UserId == query.UserId)
            .Select(p => new PortfolioDto(p.Id, p.Name, p.TotalValue))
            .ToListAsync(ct);
    }
}
followUps: What is the read model in CQRS?

followUpAnswers.Does CQRS require Event Sourcing?:
No — CQRS and Event Sourcing are independent patterns. They're often discussed together (Greg Young originally introduced them as a pair), but you can use either without the other.

CQRS without Event Sourcing (most common): Simple separation of command and query handlers, both reading/writing to the same relational database. This is the lightweight version and what most teams mean when they say "we use CQRS".

// CQRS without Event Sourcing: one database, separate handlers
// Commands: write to Portfolios table
public class CreatePortfolioHandler : IRequestHandler<CreatePortfolioCommand, int>
{
    public async Task<int> Handle(CreatePortfolioCommand cmd, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var portfolio = new Portfolio { Name = cmd.Name };
        _db.Portfolios.Add(portfolio);
        await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
        return portfolio.Id;
    }
}

// Queries: read from the same Portfolios table (with projections)
public class GetPortfolioHandler : IRequestHandler<GetPortfolioQuery, PortfolioDto>
{
    public async Task<PortfolioDto> Handle(GetPortfolioQuery q, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Portfolios
            .AsNoTracking()
            .Where(p => p.Id == q.Id)
            .Select(p => new PortfolioDto(p.Id, p.Name))
            .FirstOrDefaultAsync(ct);
}

Event Sourcing without CQRS: Store domain events as the source of truth, rebuild state from events — but use a single model for reads and writes. Unusual but possible.

Full CQRS + Event Sourcing: Commands are handled, events are appended to an event store, read models (projections) are built asynchronously from events. Complex, eventually consistent, but provides a full audit log and ability to replay history.

When to add Event Sourcing: You need a complete audit trail, temporal queries ("what was the state at time X?"), or the ability to replay and re-project events. Most CRUD apps don't need it.
followUpAnswers.What is the read model in CQRS?:
In CQRS, the read model (also called the query model or read store) is the data representation optimized specifically for reading — often denormalized, pre-aggregated, and shaped exactly for what the UI or API needs. It's separate from the write model (the normalized domain model used for commands).

Simple read model (same database, different projection):

// Write model (normalized)
public class Portfolio { public int Id; public string UserId; public string Name; }
public class Holding  { public int Id; public int PortfolioId; public decimal Quantity; public decimal Price; }

// Read model (denormalized DTO — what the dashboard actually needs)
public record PortfolioDashboardDto(
    int Id,
    string Name,
    int HoldingCount,
    decimal TotalValue,
    decimal DailyChange);

// Query handler creates the read model on the fly:
public class GetDashboardHandler : IRequestHandler<GetDashboardQuery, List<PortfolioDashboardDto>>
{
    public async Task<List<PortfolioDashboardDto>> Handle(GetDashboardQuery q, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Portfolios
            .AsNoTracking()
            .Where(p => p.UserId == q.UserId)
            .Select(p => new PortfolioDashboardDto(
                p.Id, p.Name,
                p.Holdings.Count,
                p.Holdings.Sum(h => h.Quantity * h.Price),
                0m))
            .ToListAsync(ct);
}

Separate read store (advanced): For high-scale systems, the read model can be a completely separate database (Redis cache, Elasticsearch, a denormalized SQL view, a NoSQL document store) updated asynchronously by event handlers:

// Event handler updates a Redis read model when portfolio changes
public class PortfolioChangedHandler : INotificationHandler<PortfolioChangedEvent>
{
    public async Task Handle(PortfolioChangedEvent evt, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Build read model and cache in Redis
        var dto = await BuildDashboardDtoAsync(evt.PortfolioId, ct);
        await _redis.SetAsync($"dashboard:{evt.UserId}", dto, ct);
    }
}

questions.tip: CQRS doesn't require separate databases or microservices. Even in a monolith, separating command handlers (EF Core with full tracking) from query handlers (Dapper or AsNoTracking) is valuable. Start simple — you can add separate read stores later if needed.

questions.question: What is the Singleton pattern and how is it different from Singleton lifetime in DI?
questions.answer:
The Singleton design pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point. Classic implementation:

// Thread-safe Singleton (classic pattern)
public sealed class Configuration
{
    private static readonly Lazy<Configuration> _instance =
        new Lazy<Configuration>(() => new Configuration());

    private Configuration() { /* private constructor */ }

    public static Configuration Instance => _instance.Value;

    public string ApiKey { get; private set; } = string.Empty;
}

Problems with classic Singleton:

  • Global state — makes testing hard (can't swap for test double)
  • Hides dependencies — Configuration.Instance can be called from anywhere
  • Thread safety requires careful implementation

DI Singleton lifetime — register a service as Singleton in the DI container. One instance created and reused for the app lifetime. But unlike the classic pattern:

  • The class itself has no special code — just a regular class
  • Instance is managed and disposed by the container
  • Swappable in tests (inject different implementation)
  • Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit

Prefer DI Singleton over classic Singleton pattern. I only use classic Singleton for code that truly needs to be accessible without DI (rare).
followUps: What is the difference between Singleton and static class?
Thread safety of Singleton services in ASP.NET Core?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Singleton:
Both provide a single, globally accessible instance, but they differ fundamentally in design, flexibility, and testability:

Static classSingleton (DI)
InstanceNo instance — all members are staticOne managed instance via DI container
InterfacesCannot implement interfacesCan implement interfaces — swappable
InheritanceCannot inherit or be inheritedNormal class — full inheritance
TestabilityCannot mock — tightly coupledMock via interface — fully testable
Lifetime managementLives forever — no disposeDisposed by DI container on shutdown
StateGlobal static state — concurrency issuesInstance state — thread safety your responsibility
Lazy initType initializer (not lazily configurable)DI container controls instantiation timing
// Static class: fine for stateless utility methods
public static class StringExtensions
{
    public static string ToSlug(this string s)
        => s.ToLower().Replace(" ", "-");
}

// Static class: BAD for stateful or I/O-dependent operations
public static class UserCache  // AVOID
{
    private static readonly Dictionary<int, User> _cache = new();
    public static User? Get(int id) => _cache.GetValueOrDefault(id);
    // Cannot mock, cannot dispose, no DI, hidden dependency
}

// DI Singleton: correct for shared stateful services
public class UserCacheService : IUserCacheService
{
    private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;
    public UserCacheService(IMemoryCache cache) => _cache = cache;
    // Mockable, disposable, testable
}
builder.Services.AddSingleton<IUserCacheService, UserCacheService>();
followUpAnswers.Thread safety of Singleton services in A:
Singleton services in ASP.NET Core are shared across all concurrent requests — multiple threads access the same instance simultaneously. If the singleton holds mutable state, you must make it thread-safe.

Common thread-safety violations:

// DANGEROUS: mutable Dictionary shared across requests
public class PortfolioCacheService  // registered as Singleton
{
    private readonly Dictionary<int, Portfolio> _cache = new();

    public void Set(int id, Portfolio p) => _cache[id] = p;  // NOT thread-safe!
    public Portfolio? Get(int id) => _cache.GetValueOrDefault(id);  // NOT thread-safe!
}

// SAFE options:

// Option 1: ConcurrentDictionary
private readonly ConcurrentDictionary<int, Portfolio> _cache = new();
public void Set(int id, Portfolio p) => _cache[id] = p;  // thread-safe
public Portfolio? Get(int id) => _cache.GetValueOrDefault(id);  // thread-safe

// Option 2: IMemoryCache (already thread-safe)
public class PortfolioCacheService
{
    private readonly IMemoryCache _cache;
    public PortfolioCacheService(IMemoryCache cache) => _cache = cache;

    public void Set(int id, Portfolio p)
        => _cache.Set(id, p, TimeSpan.FromMinutes(10));  // thread-safe
    public Portfolio? Get(int id)
        => _cache.TryGetValue(id, out Portfolio? p) ? p : null;
}

// Option 3: Immutable data — safest approach
// Build the data once at startup, never mutate
public class QuestionService  // Singleton
{
    private readonly IReadOnlyList<Question> _questions;  // set once, never changed
    public QuestionService(IConfiguration cfg)
    {
        _questions = LoadQuestionsFromJson(cfg);  // loaded at construction
    }
    public IReadOnlyList<Question> GetAll() => _questions;  // read-only, always safe
}

Rule of thumb for Singleton services: prefer immutable state (built at startup, never changed) or thread-safe collections. Avoid non-thread-safe mutable state entirely. If you need request-specific state, use Scoped services instead.

questions.tip: Singleton services must be thread-safe in ASP.NET Core — multiple requests run concurrently and share the same instance. Avoid mutable state in singletons. If you need per-request state, use Scoped services.
